content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660         322-5660              322-5901             322-6441              322-6444

May 14, 1981


Clayton P. Roche
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
350 McAllister Street
San Francisco, CA  94102

Re: Your Letter re Opinion No. 81-416

Dear Mr. Roche:

     Thank you for your letter requesting the Commission's
input for your consideration in developing Opinion No.
81-416.  Barbara Milman has asked me to respond to you.
Your letter indicates that the Attorney General's opinion
has been requested on the following question:

     "Is it a conflict of interest for a County Super-
visor who is also a licensed Real Estate Broker to act
for either the buyer or seller in a real estate trans-
action which is contingent on County approval of such
matters as compliance with Building Codes, obtaining
conditional use permits, rezoning, etc?

     "This question presupposes that the Supervisor
will not in any way attempt to influence any County
employee, will not participate in any application for
such County approval and will, if the matter comes
before the Board of Supervisors, disclose his interest
in the transaction and disqualify himself from voting."

     The conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (Government Code Sections 87100-87313) relate to
disqualification.  The Act does not prohibit other activities
as long as the public official disqualifies himself or herself
from deciding or participating in the making of a decision
which will affect his/her financial interest.  Participation
in this context is broadly defined, and includes such things
as chairing meetings and lobbying other Board members.

Clayton P. Roche
May 14, 1981
Page Two


     Based upon your statement of the issue, we conclude
that there would be no violation of the Political Reform Act
in undertaking the activities described. If you have any
other information or any further questions, please advise us
and we will be happy to consider them.

     Obviously, other provisions of law or ethics may cause
a contrary conclusion, as was found in your recent Opinion
No. 80-1204, regarding the ethical obligations of attorneys.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division
                                        (916) 322-5901

RE:kp

State of California

Department of Justice

George Deukmejian
(PRONOUNCED DUKE-MAY-GIN)

Attorney General
(415) 557-1586

350 McALLISTER STREET
SAN FRANCISCO 94102
(415) 557-2544

FPPC

May 6 11 37 AM '81

May 4, 1981

Ms. Barbara Milman
Conflict of Interest Division Chief
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Opinion No. 81-416

Dear Ms. Milman:

Our opinion has been requested on the following
question:

Is it a conflict of interest for a County
Supervisor who is also a licensed Real
Estate Broker to act for either the buyer
or seller in a real estate transaction
which is contingent on County approval of
such matters as compliance with Building
Codes, obtaining conditional use permits,
rezoning, etc.?

This question presupposes that the Super-
visor will not in any way attempt to
influence any County employee, will not
participate in any application for such
County approval and will, if the matter
comes before the Board of Supervisors,
disclose his interest in the transaction
and disqualify himself from voting.

Pursuant to our policy to request the views of
interested parties and associations, you are invited to submit
whatever views you may desire on the question presented. Also,
if you do not wish to submit any views, please so advise us.

My administrative deadline for the preparation of a
response is June 5, 1981. Therefore, your prompt attention
to this letter will be appreciated.

Very truly yours,

GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN
Attorney General

[signature]
CLAYTOM P. ROCHE
Deputy Attorney General

CPR:lv

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a County Supervisor who is also a licensed Real Estate Broker may act as an agent for either party in a real estate transaction that requires County approval, under the presumption that the Supervisor will not influence County employees or participate in the approval process and will disqualify himself if the matter comes before the Board of Supervisors.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains both an incoming request letter from Attorney General (May 4, 1981) and an FPPC response letter (May 14, 1981). Extracted the incoming request portion as the primary document. The FPPC response is fragmentary/partially legible but appears to indicate no conflict exists under stated conditions. Confidence reduced due to image quality and fragment visibility in response section.

embedding.summary: The California Attorney General's office (George Deukmejian) submitted a request to the FPPC seeking guidance on whether a County Supervisor's dual role as a licensed Real Estate Broker creates a conflict of interest when transacting in matters requiring County approval, with safeguards including non-interference and disqualification from voting.
